facts.label: SCOUTED FROM ENGLAND
facts.story: Coach René Simões knew Jamaica needed an edge, so in 1997 he went looking for Jamaican heritage in the English lower leagues. Paul Hall, Fitzroy Simpson, Deon Burton, and Wimbledon's Robbie Earle all qualified through family roots and joined the Reggae Boyz. Jamaicans nicknamed the England-raised imports 'UB40s,' a wink at the British reggae band, and three of them started the country's historic first World Cup match.

facts.label: THE DAY THE COUNTRY STOPPED
facts.story: Jamaica sealed its ticket to France with a 2–2 draw against El Salvador in the final round of qualifying, becoming the first English-speaking Caribbean nation ever to reach the World Cup. The celebration was so large that the government declared the following day a national holiday, with reggae music and impromptu parades filling the streets of Kingston long before a single ball had been kicked in France.

1998.headline: First Goal, First Win

annotations.label: EARLE'S HISTORIC HEADER
annotations.story: In Jamaica's first-ever World Cup match, Wimbledon's Robbie Earle rose to head home in first-half stoppage time — the first goal in Jamaican World Cup history. Croatia had already led through Mario Stanić, and second-half strikes from Robert Prosinečki and Davor Šuker sealed a 3–1 win for the eventual semi-finalists.

annotations.label: OUTCLASSED IN PARIS
annotations.story: At the Parc des Princes, Jamaica ran into an Argentina side chasing perfection. Ariel Ortega struck twice and Gabriel Batistuta added a hat-trick, the last from the penalty spot, as Argentina cruised 5–0 — still Jamaica's heaviest defeat on the world stage.

annotations.label: WHITMORE WRITES HIS NAME IN
annotations.story: With qualification already gone, Theodore Whitmore delivered Jamaica's proudest World Cup moment in Lyon, scoring in the 39th and 54th minutes to beat Japan 2–1 — the country's only win at a World Cup finals. Masashi Nakayama's late reply for Japan couldn't stop the party back home.

1998.homeColors: black shirt with gold and green trim echoing the Jamaican flag — the widely celebrated 1998 Kappa kit
